title: The four-color theorem and an instanton invariant for spatial graphs II
speaker: Tomasz Mrowka
abstract: Given a trivalent graph embedded in 3-space, we associate to it an instanton homology group, which is a finite-dimensional $\mathbf{Z}/2$ vector space. The main result about the instanton homology is a non-vanishing theorem, proved using techniques from 3-dimensional topology: if the graph is bridgeless, its instanton homology is non-zero.

proof_stages.label: Recap of the spatial-web invariant and rank conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by recalling the instanton Floer invariant J-sharp of embedded trivalent spatial graphs and the conjecture that, for planar bridgeless graphs, its rank counts Tait colorings.

proof_stages.label: Foams as orbifold cobordisms with dotted insertions
proof_stages.summary: The invariant is promoted from a vector-space assignment to a functorial theory by defining four-dimensional foams as orbifold singular loci, allowing web boundaries, constructing cobordism maps by anti-self-dual Yang--Mills counts, and adding dots as cohomology insertions on moduli spaces.

proof_stages.label: Unknot and theta module computations
proof_stages.summary: The dotted product foams yield polynomial-algebra actions on J-sharp, with the unknot computed as a quotient by a cubic relation and the theta graph governed by the homology-ring relations of a flag manifold.

proof_stages.label: Local foam simplification calculus
proof_stages.summary: The basic module computations are used to justify local identities for foam-induced maps, including sphere removal, neck cutting, dot migration, special dotted sphere relations, and cancellation of paired tetrahedral points.

proof_stages.label: Web relations from foam calculus
proof_stages.summary: The foam identities imply computational relations for J-sharp of webs, notably the bigon direct-sum relation, the triangle isomorphism, and the square direct-sum relation, which parallel the behavior of Tait colorings.

proof_stages.label: Foam-generated classes and lower-bound method
proof_stages.summary: Foams with only one web boundary define classes in J-sharp, leading to a proposed combinatorial quotient by invisible foams and to a method, effective in planar half-space settings, for producing independent classes such as the lower bound for the dodecahedron.

proof_stages.label: Exact triangles and the octahedron of local web modifications
proof_stages.summary: For three local web modifications, natural foam cobordisms produce exact triangles; barred variants give four related exact triangles that assemble into an octahedron with exact and commutative faces.

proof_stages.label: Tutte relation, square homology, and bounds
proof_stages.summary: The octahedral square is compared with Tutte's characterization of Tait colorings, yielding differential complexes whose homology controls the desired relation and giving concrete dodecahedron bounds that indicate both promise and remaining obstacles.

examples.name: Torus with a pair of attached disks as a sample foam
examples.note: The speaker lists simple singular two-complexes, including a torus with attached disks, to make the abstract local definition of a foam visually concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He lists a torus with a pair of disks attached as one legitimate foam picture, but does not analyze or manipulate it.

examples.name: Bipartite planar graphs reducible by local moves
examples.note: Bipartite planar graphs are cited as a family where the bigon, triangle, and square relations reduce the graph to a trivial one, giving evidence for the Tait-coloring rank conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: He states that bipartite planar graphs can be reduced to a trivial graph by the bigon/triangle/square moves, but gives no concrete reduction or computation for one.

passes.reason: A systematic reducible family used as evidence for the conjecture, not to refute one.

examples.name: Dodecahedron graph
examples.note: The dodecahedron is used as a test case whose foam-generated pairings give a lower bound and whose octahedral/Tutte-square computation exposes limitations of the stronger exactness hope.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the foam/octahedron machinery to the dodecahedron, discussing generated foams, simplifications of related graphs, homology computations, and resulting bounds such as 58, 70, and 68.

passes.reason: The highly symmetric minimal girth-five cubic planar graph is used as the hard case undermining the strong square-exactness hope.
The dodecahedral graph is the highly symmetric minimal cubic planar girth-five case and is used as the hard obstruction to the hoped-for strong Tut exactness.
The regular dodecahedral graph is highly symmetric and attains the cubic-planar girth bound, and is used as the hard case undermining the strong exact-square conjecture.
The dodecahedral graph is a highly regular Platonic cubic planar graph and a girth-5/no-small-face boundary case, and it is used as the hard obstruction to the strong Tut exactness expectation.

examples.name: Usual knot-theoretic skein exact sequence comparison
examples.note: The familiar exact sequence for three knots related by a local crossing change is invoked only as a contrast with the SO(3) web theory, where barred variants enter instead.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes the usual knot-theoretic skein exact sequence only as a comparison with the SO(3) web situation, without working through that skein sequence itself.
